1.pericope_label: A Fountain for Sin and Uncleanness
1.synthesis: The outpouring of grief in 12:10–14 flows into the provision of cleansing: “On that day there shall be a fountain opened for the house of David and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, to cleanse them from sin and uncleanness (lachataʾt ulniddah).” (Zechariah 13:1). Niddah is the specific term for ritual impurity associated with menstruation (Leviticus 15:19–30), and by extension with the most persistent, cyclically renewing forms of impurity. The fountain (maqor) opened for this cleansing is inexhaustible and always available — the permanent solution to what the Day of Atonement addressed annually. Then the idols and prophets are cut off: on that day YHWH of armies will cut off the names of idols from the land; they shall no more be remembered. The false prophets (who prophesy by the spirit of impurity, ruach ha’tumah) will be removed; their own parents will pierce them if they prophesy. The false prophet will be ashamed of his visions, will not put on the hairy mantle to deceive, but will claim to be a farmer, denying prophetic identity entirely.
1.historical_context: The hairy mantle (aderet se’ar) was the prophet’s distinctive clothing (Elijah’s, 2 Kings 1:8; cf. John the Baptist’s camel-hair garment, Matthew 3:4). The false prophet’s denial of prophetic identity while showing wounds (“I was wounded in the house of my friends”, v.6 — possibly self-inflicted cultic wounds of Baal prophets, 1 Kings 18:28) reveals the depth of post-prophetic disorientation. The cleansing fountain drawing on Ezekiel’s life-giving river (Ezekiel 47:1–12) and the new covenant promise of comprehensive forgiveness (Jeremiah 31:34: “I will forgive their iniquity and remember their sin no more”).
1.literary_structure: The fountain (v.1) is the positive provision whose negative corollary is the elimination of idols and false prophecy (vv.2–6). The same “on that day” (’bayom hahu’) that opens the cleansing fountain closes false prophetic structures: the day of mourning-and-cleansing is also the day of idolatry’s end. The section closes on the ironic image of the false prophet who denies his own calling (v.6) — the inverse of the genuine prophet who can no longer hold back the word (Jeremiah 20:9).
1.theological_application: The fountain for sin and uncleanness as the permanent solution to the community’s persistent impurity models the new covenant’s decisive character. Old covenant provision addressed impurity cyclically (annual Day of Atonement, regular purification rituals); the fountain opened “on that day” is permanent and inexhaustible. The elimination of false prophecy alongside idolatry shows that false speech about YHWH is as serious a defilement as false images of him — both distort the covenantal relationship at its roots.
1.christology: The fountain opened for sin and uncleanness is identified in John 19:34 with the blood and water from Christ’s pierced side — the two fluids of purification (water for niddah impurity, blood for atonement of sin) flowing from the body of the pierced one. John’s comment (“he who saw it has borne witness”, John 19:35) marks this as a theological observation. The fountain of Zechariah 13:1 is thus opened by the piercing of Zechariah 12:10 — the two passages form a single event: wound and cure, piercing and fountain, mourning and cleansing.

7.synthesis: “Awake, O sword, against my shepherd, against the man who stands next to me (gever ’amiti), declares YHWH of armies. Strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered; I will turn my hand against the little ones.” (Zechariah 13:7). The shepherd struck here is not the worthless shepherd of ch.11 who deserved the sword; he is the shepherd who is YHWH’s intimate associate (“my companion,” ’amiti, the word used for the fellow-covenant-member in Leviticus 19:15–18). YHWH himself calls the sword against his own shepherd. Two thirds of the land will be cut off and perish; one third will remain. This third YHWH will put in the fire, refine as silver is refined, test as gold is tested. They will call on his name; he will answer them; he will say, “They are my people”; and they will say, “YHWH is my God” (Zechariah 13:9). The double exchange of covenant formula (“my people” / “my God”) is the goal of the entire painful process: the scattering, the refining, the loss of two thirds — all produce the covenant relationship restored to its simplest, most complete form.
7.historical_context: Jesus cites this passage at Gethsemane: “You will all fall away because of me this night, for it is written, ‘I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered.’” (Matthew 26:31; Mark 14:27). The gever ’amiti — man of my company, intimate associate — is extraordinary: a human being (gever) who is simultaneously YHWH’s intimate fellow. The two-thirds/one-third division reflects covenant discipline patterns and may have near-term application to 70 CE’s catastrophe, with the one-third remnant being the messianic community.
7.literary_structure: Three verses, two movements: sword against shepherd and scattering (v.7) then remnant refined and covenant formula (vv.8–9). The brevity mirrors the structural brevity of the worthless shepherd oracle (11:15–17), but the emotional and theological weight is entirely different: this shepherd is YHWH’s own; the striking is YHWH’s own act; the purpose is the purified covenant people. The covenant formula of v.9 (“they are my people / YHWH is my God”) echoes Hosea 2:23 and reverses Hosea’s “not my people.”
7.theological_application: YHWH calling the sword against his own shepherd models the theology of the cross: the suffering is not accidental or the work of enemies alone; it is the will of YHWH expressed through human instruments. Isaiah 53:10 (“it was the will of YHWH to crush him; he has put him to grief”) is the closest parallel. The one-third refined in fire and emerging as covenant people shows that the purpose of the shepherd-striking is not destruction but purification — the cross is not the end of the story but the means to covenant restoration.
7.christology: Jesus’ direct citation of Zechariah 13:7 at Gethsemane (Matthew 26:31) makes the identification explicit: he is the shepherd YHWH strikes; the disciples are the scattered sheep. The gever ’amiti — the man who is YHWH’s intimate companion — is the most precise OT articulation of what the NT calls the incarnate Son: a human being (gever) who stands in the relationship of divine fellowship (’amiti). The covenant formula of v.9 describes the church: those who were “not my people” who through the shepherd-striking become “my people” and say “YHWH is my God.”
